content:
# Genesis Chapter 9 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 5v–6r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_009_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_009_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 6–7 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**9:1** And God blessed [וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים] Noah [אֶת-נֹחַ] and his sons [וְאֶת-בָּנָיו], and he said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם]: Be fruitful and multiply [פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ] and fill the earth [וּמִלְאוּ אֶת-הָאָרֶץ].

**9:2** And the fear of you [וּמוֹרַאֲכֶם] and the dread of you [וְחִתְּכֶם] shall be upon every living thing of the earth [יִהְיֶה עַל כָּל-חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ] and upon every flying-thing of the heavens [וְעַל כָּל-עוֹף הַשָּׁמָיִם]; in everything the ground swarms with [בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּרְמֹשׂ הָאֲדָמָה] and all the fish of the sea [וּבְכָל-דְּגֵי הַיָּם] — into your hand they are given [בְּיֶדְכֶם נִתָּנוּ].

**9:3** Every moving thing that lives [כָּל-רֶמֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר הוּא-חַי] — to you it shall be for food [לָכֶם יִהְיֶה לְאָכְלָה]; as the green plant [כְּיֶרֶק עֵשֶׂב] I have given to you all [נָתַתִּי לָכֶם אֶת-כֹּל].

**9:4** But flesh [אַךְ-בָּשָׂר] with its life [בְּנַפְשׁוֹ] — its blood [דָמוֹ] — you shall not eat [לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ].

**9:5** And indeed your blood [וְאַךְ אֶת-דִּמְכֶם], for your lives [לְנַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם], I will require [אֶדְרֹשׁ]; from the hand of every living thing [מִיַּד כָּל-חַיָּה] I will require it [אֶדְרְשֶׁנּוּ], and from the hand of the adam [וּמִיַּד הָאָדָם], from the hand of each man — his brother [מִיַּד אִישׁ אָחִיו] — I will require the life of the adam [אֶדְרֹשׁ אֶת-נֶפֶשׁ הָאָדָם].

**9:6** Whoever sheds the blood of the adam [שֹׁפֵךְ דַּם הָאָדָם], by the adam shall his blood be shed [בָּאָדָם דָּמוֹ יִשָּׁפֵךְ]; for in the image of God [כִּי בְּצֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים] he made the adam [עָשָׂה אֶת-הָאָדָם].

**9:7** And you [וְאַתֶּם] — be fruitful [פְּרוּ] and multiply [וּרְבוּ], swarm upon the earth [שִׁרְצוּ בָאָרֶץ] and multiply upon it [וּרְבוּ-בָהּ].

**9:8** And God spoke [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים] to Noah [אֶל-נֹחַ] and to his sons with him [וְאֶל-בָּנָיו אִתּוֹ] saying [לֵאמֹר]:

**9:9** And I [וַאֲנִי] — behold [הִנְנִי] — am establishing my covenant [מֵקִים אֶת-בְּרִיתִי] with you [אִתְּכֶם] and with your seed after you [וְאֶת-זַרְעֲכֶם אַחֲרֵיכֶם],

**9:10** and with every living being [וְאֵת כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ הַחַיָּה] that is with you [אֲשֶׁר אִתְּכֶם] — of the flying-things [בָּעוֹף] of the beasts [בַּבְּהֵמָה] and of every living thing of the earth with you [וּבְכָל-חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ אִתְּכֶם] — of all who went out of the ark [מִכֹּל יֹצְאֵי הַתֵּבָה], to every living thing of the earth [לְכֹל חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ].

**9:11** And I will establish my covenant with you [וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי אִתְּכֶם] — and all flesh shall not again be cut off [וְלֹא-יִכָּרֵת כָּל-בָּשָׂר עוֹד] by the waters of the flood [מִמֵּי הַמַּבּוּל] — and there shall not again be a flood [וְלֹא-יִהְיֶה עוֹד מַבּוּל] to destroy the earth [לְשַׁחֵת הָאָרֶץ].

**9:12** And God said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים]: This is the sign of the covenant [זֹאת אוֹת-הַבְּרִית] that I am giving [אֲשֶׁר-אֲנִי נֹתֵן] between me and between you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֵיכֶם] and between every living being [וּבֵין כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה] that is with you [אֲשֶׁר אִתְּכֶם] for generations of eternity [לְדֹרֹת עוֹלָם]:

**9:13** My bow [אֶת-קַשְׁתִּי] I have set [נָתַתִּי] in the cloud [בֶּעָנָן], and it shall be for a sign of the covenant [וְהָיְתָה לְאוֹת בְּרִית] between me and between the earth [בֵּינִי וּבֵין הָאָרֶץ].

**9:14** And it shall come to pass [וְהָיָה] when I bring clouds [בְּעַנְנִי עָנָן] over the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ], that the bow shall be seen [וְנִרְאֲתָה הַקֶּשֶׁת] in the cloud [בֶּעָנָן].

**9:15** And I will remember my covenant [וְזָכַרְתִּי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי] which is between me and between you [אֲשֶׁר בֵּינִי וּבֵינֵיכֶם] and between every living being [וּבֵין כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה] of all flesh [בְּכָל-בָּשָׂר], and the waters shall not again become [וְלֹא-יִהְיֶה עוֹד הַמַּיִם] a flood [לְמַבּוּל] to destroy all flesh [לְשַׁחֵת כָּל-בָּשָׂר].

**9:16** And the bow shall be in the cloud [וְהָיְתָה הַקֶּשֶׁת בֶּעָנָן], and I will look upon it [וּרְאִיתִיהָ] to remember [לִזְכֹּר] the everlasting covenant [בְּרִית עוֹלָם] between God and between every living being [בֵּין אֱלֹהִים וּבֵין כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה] of all flesh [בְּכָל-בָּשָׂר] that is upon the earth [אֲשֶׁר עַל-הָאָרֶץ].

**9:17** And God said to Noah [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים אֶל-נֹחַ]: This is the sign of the covenant [זֹאת אוֹת-הַבְּרִית] that I have established [אֲשֶׁר הֲקִמֹתִי] between me and between all flesh [בֵּינִי וּבֵין כָּל-בָּשָׂר] that is upon the earth [אֲשֶׁר עַל-הָאָרֶץ].

**9:18** And the sons of Noah who went out of the ark [וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי-נֹחַ הַיֹּצְאִים מִן-הַתֵּבָה] were Shem [שֵׁם] and Ham [חָם] and Japheth [וָיָפֶת]; and Ham [וְחָם] — he was the father of Canaan [הוּא אֲבִי כְנָעַן].

**9:19** These three were the sons of Noah [שְׁלֹשָׁה אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי-נֹחַ], and from these [וּמֵאֵלֶּה] the whole earth was scattered [נָפְצָה כָל-הָאָרֶץ].

**9:20** And Noah began [וַיָּחֶל נֹחַ] — a man of the ground he was [אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה] — and he planted [וַיִּטַּע] a vineyard [כָּרֶם].

**9:21** And he drank from the wine [וַיֵּשְׁתְּ מִן-הַיַּיִן] and became drunk [וַיִּשְׁכָּר], and he uncovered himself [וַיִּתְגַּל] within his tent [בְּתוֹךְ אָהֳלֹה].

**9:22** And Ham saw [וַיַּרְא חָם] — the father of Canaan [אֲבִי כְנָעַן] — the nakedness of his father [אֵת עֶרְוַת אָבִיו], and he told [וַיַּגֵּד] to his two brothers [לִשְׁנֵי-אֶחָיו] outside [בַּחוּץ].

**9:23** And Shem took [וַיִּקַּח שֵׁם] and Japheth [וָיֶפֶת] the garment [אֶת-הַשִּׂמְלָה], and they put [it] upon both their shoulders [וַיָּשִׂימוּ עַל-שְׁכֶם שְׁנֵיהֶם], and they walked backward [וַיֵּלְכוּ אֲחֹרַנִּית], and they covered [וַיְכַסּוּ] the nakedness of their father [אֵת עֶרְוַת אָבִיהֶם]; and their faces were backward [וּפְנֵיהֶם אֲחֹרַנִּית], and the nakedness of their father they did not see [וְעֶרְוַת אֲבִיהֶם לֹא רָאוּ].

**9:24** And Noah awoke [וַיִּיקֶץ נֹחַ] from his wine [מִיֵּינוֹ], and he knew [וַיֵּדַע] what his youngest son [אֵת אֲשֶׁר-עָשָׂה לוֹ בְּנוֹ הַקָּטָן] had done to him.

**9:25** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Cursed be Canaan [אָרוּר כְּנָעַן] — a slave of slaves [עֶבֶד עֲבָדִים] shall he be [יִהְיֶה] to his brothers [לְאֶחָיו].

**9:26** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Blessed be YHWH [בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה], the God of Shem [אֱלֹהֵי שֵׁם]; and Canaan [וִיהִי כְנַעַן] shall be a slave to them [עֶבֶד לָמוֹ].

**9:27** God shall enlarge Japheth [יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים לְיֶפֶת], and he shall dwell [וְיִשְׁכֹּן] in the tents of Shem [בְּאָהֳלֵי-שֵׁם]; and Canaan [וִיהִי כְנַעַן] shall be a slave to them [עֶבֶד לָמוֹ].

**9:28** And Noah lived [וַיְחִי-נֹחַ] after the flood [אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל] three hundred and fifty years [שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַחֲמִשִּׁים שָׁנָה].

**9:29** And all the days of Noah [וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל-יְמֵי-נֹחַ] were nine hundred and fifty years [תְּשַׁע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַחֲמִשִּׁים שָׁנָה] and he died [וַיָּמֹת].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 9:1 — פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ: "Be Fruitful and Multiply" — The Re-Creation Mandate

וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ וְאֶת-בָּנָיו וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ וּמִלְאוּ אֶת-הָאָרֶץ — "And God blessed Noah and his sons, and he said to them: Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth."

The verbal echo of Genesis 1:28 is exact and deliberate: פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ וּמִלְאוּ אֶת-הָאָרֶץ — the identical triple command issued to the adam and his wife at creation is now re-issued to Noah and his sons. This is not an allusion; it is a verbatim repetition, the clearest possible signal that Noah stands at a new creation. The theological structure is:

| Gen 1:28 | Gen 9:1 |
|---|---|
| God blessed them | God blessed Noah and his sons |
| Be fruitful and multiply | Be fruitful and multiply |
| Fill the earth | Fill the earth |
| Have dominion over the creatures | [Now modified: the creatures will fear you] |

The post-flood world is a second creation with a crucial modification: what was in 1:28 a mandate for harmonious dominion becomes in 9:2 a regime of fear and dread. The same commission, different relational terms. This difference is theologically significant: the pre-flood world is the world of created harmony; the post-flood world is the world of the fall fully realized in the human-animal relationship.

**LXX 9:1:** αὐξάνεσθε καὶ πληθύνεσθε καὶ πληρώσατε τὴν γῆν — "Increase and multiply and fill the earth." The LXX uses αὐξάνω ("to increase, grow") for פָּרָה ("to be fruitful"), and πληθύνω ("to multiply") for רָבָה — the same LXX verbs used in 1:28, faithfully maintaining the echo.

---

### On 9:2 — מוֹרַאֲכֶם וְחִתְּכֶם: "Your Fear and Your Dread"

וּמוֹרַאֲכֶם וְחִתְּכֶם יִהְיֶה עַל כָּל-חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ — "And the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every living thing of the earth."

Two distinct Hebrew nouns are joined here:
- **מוֹרָא** (mora') — fear, awe, terror; from the root יָרֵא (yare', "to fear, revere"). Used of reverential fear as well as terror.
- **חִתָּה** (chitah) — dread, terror, dismay; from the root חָתַת (chatat, "to be shattered, terrified"). A stronger, more visceral term of being broken with fear.

The pairing is a hendiadys of intensification: "fear-and-dread," total creaturely terror before humanity. In Genesis 1:26-28, the human's relationship to the animal world is expressed with the verbs רָדָה (radah, "to have dominion, rule") and כָּבַשׁ (kavash, "to subdue") — powerful verbs, but not terror-language. In 9:2, the post-flood relationship is defined not by royal dominion (which implies order and governance) but by primal fear — the instinctive flight-response of prey before predator. The granting of permission to eat animals (v. 3) explains this shift: once humans become predators of animals, the animals will fear them. The Edenic harmony of shared herbivory is over.

**"In everything the ground swarms with" (בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּרְמֹשׂ הָאֲדָמָה):** The verb רָמַשׂ (ramas, "to creep, swarm") echoes the creation vocabulary of Genesis 1:24-25 (the רֶמֶשׂ, the creeping things). The full range of animate life — creatures of earth, sky, and sea — is placed under the human dread-regime. The taxonomy here (every living thing of the earth / every flying-thing of the heavens / everything that swarms / all fish of the sea) mirrors the creation categories of Genesis 1.

---

### On 9:3 — כְּיֶרֶק עֵשֶׂב נָתַתִּי לָכֶם אֶת-כֹּל: Meat Permitted

כָּל-רֶמֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר הוּא-חַי לָכֶם יִהְיֶה לְאָכְלָה כְּיֶרֶק עֵשֶׂב נָתַתִּי לָכֶם אֶת-כֹּל — "Every moving thing that lives — to you it shall be for food; as the green plant I have given to you all."

In Genesis 1:29-30, food was exclusively plant-based: God gave "every green plant" to humans and every plant to animals. No animal was killed for food in the pre-flood world according to the biblical narrative's theological idealization. Here the permission to eat meat is explicitly granted for the first time, explicitly modeled on the earlier grant of plants: "as the green plant, I have given to you all." The grant is total and undiscriminating — "every moving thing that lives" — with the single exception stated in the immediately following verse: blood. The theological logic is: the flood has permanently altered the human-animal relationship (v. 2); that alteration is now ratified in dietary permission. The world after the flood operates under different rules.

**The limits on this permission:** The permission is modified by v. 4 (blood prohibition) and later by the Mosaic food laws (Leviticus 11; Deuteronomy 14). The Noahic permission is universal (given to all humanity via Noah); the Levitical restrictions are particular (given only to Israel). The Noahic code thus functions as the ethical minimum for all human civilization, while the Levitical code is the additional particular obligation of the covenant people.

---

### On 9:4 — אַךְ-בָּשָׂר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ: The Blood Prohibition

אַךְ-בָּשָׂר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ — literally: "But flesh in its life — its blood — you shall not eat."

This verse is one of the most grammatically compressed in the Torah. Its literal structure is:

> **אַךְ-בָּשָׂר** (but / only — flesh)
> **בְּנַפְשׁוֹ** (in its life / in its soul — modifying flesh)
> **דָמוֹ** (its blood — appositive to נַפְשׁוֹ)
> **לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ** (you shall not eat)

The construction sets up an apposition: the נֶפֶשׁ (nephesh, life-force, soul) of the animal resides in its blood. דָּם (dam, blood) and נֶפֶשׁ (nephesh, life) are identified as equivalent. This identification is made explicit in Leviticus 17:11: "for the life [נֶפֶשׁ] of the flesh is in the blood [בַּדָּם]" and Deuteronomy 12:23: "for the blood — it is the life [הַנֶּפֶשׁ]." The prohibition is therefore not merely dietary but metaphysical: blood is the carrier of the life-force; consuming blood is consuming the life-substance of the creature.

**Translation difficulty:** The Hebrew is elliptical. The EMET rendering "But flesh with its life — its blood — you shall not eat" represents the appositive structure directly: "its life" and "its blood" are in apposition, the dash marking the explicating relationship. Many translations render this as "you shall not eat flesh with its life, that is, its blood" — which is essentially the same analysis, made explicit. The EMET translation preserves the compression.

**LXX 9:4:** πλὴν κρέας ἐν αἵματι ψυχῆς οὐ φάγεσθε — "But flesh with blood of life [= living blood] you shall not eat." The LXX renders נַפְשׁוֹ (its life) as ψυχῆς (of life), treating it as a genitive modifying αἵματι (blood): "blood of life" rather than the appositive "its life — its blood." This is a legitimate but different syntactical reading that loses the identification of blood and nephesh and instead produces "you shall not eat flesh in [= with] the blood of life."

**The Noahic prohibition and the apostolic decree (Acts 15):** The Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:28-29) issued a minimum Gentile obligation that included abstaining from blood — almost certainly understood as the Noahic blood prohibition being re-affirmed as the universal ethical floor applicable to all humanity, not only Israel.

---

### On 9:5-6 — The Demand for Blood Accounting: Chiasm and Capital Punishment

**9:5 — אֶדְרֹשׁ: "I will require"**

The verb דָּרַשׁ (darash, "to seek, inquire, require, hold accountable") is used three times in v. 5:

1. אֶת-דִּמְכֶם לְנַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם אֶדְרֹשׁ — "for your lives I will require your blood"
2. מִיַּד כָּל-חַיָּה אֶדְרְשֶׁנּוּ — "from the hand of every living thing I will require it"
3. מִיַּד אִישׁ אָחִיו אֶדְרֹשׁ אֶת-נֶפֶשׁ הָאָדָם — "from the hand of each man — his brother — I will require the life of the adam"

The triple repetition establishes the principle of universal blood-accountability. God himself is the one who holds this account. The phrase "from the hand of each man — his brother" is striking: the killer of a human is killing his own brother. The fraternal relationship (אָח, "brother") is invoked not as a limit (only brothers) but as a qualification of what every human is: every person is another person's brother. Killing any human is fratricide.

**9:6 — The Chiasm**

שֹׁפֵךְ דַּם הָאָדָם בָּאָדָם דָּמוֹ יִשָּׁפֵךְ — this verse in Hebrew is a perfect chiasm:

```
A   שֹׁפֵךְ   (shed)
  B   דַּם   (blood)
    C   הָאָדָם   (the adam)
    C'  בָּאָדָם   (by the adam)
  B'  דָּמוֹ   (his blood)
A'  יִשָּׁפֵךְ   (shall be shed)
```

The center of the chiasm (C/C') pivots on the word הָאָדָם / בָּאָדָם — the same word, the same adam, appearing on both sides of the center. The one who sheds is the adam; the one by whom blood is shed is also the adam. The sentence folds on itself: the adam is both subject of the crime and instrument of the consequence. The chiastic structure is not decorative; it enacts the principle: blood-for-blood, adam-for-adam, the structure of justice mirrored in the structure of the sentence.

**"For in the image of God he made the adam":** This is the theological ground of the law. The prohibition against killing humans is not merely pragmatic (social order requires it) or retributive (blood calls for blood as an instinctive demand). The ground is ontological: the adam is the image of God (בְּצֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה אֶת-הָאָדָם). To kill a human is to attack the image of God in the world. The severity of the prohibition is commensurate with the dignity of the being. This verse is the biblical foundation for the unique status of human life: humans may not be killed because they bear the divine image.

**Note on translation — בָּאָדָם:** The preposition בְּ (be) here means "by" (the agent of the passive verb) — "by the adam shall his blood be shed." This is the standard instrument/agent usage of בְּ with passives. Some translations render it "for/on account of the adam," but "by the adam" is the more natural reading of בְּ with a passive verb: human judicial execution is the instrument of divine blood-accountability.

**LXX 9:6:** ὁ ἐκχέων αἷμα ἀνθρώπου ἀντὶ τοῦ αἵματος αὐτοῦ ἐκχυθήσεται — "The one who pours out the blood of a man — in return for his blood it shall be poured out." The LXX renders בָּאָדָם as ἀντὶ τοῦ αἵματος αὐτοῦ ("in return for his blood") — reading it as a retributive formula ("blood in return for blood") rather than the agentive "by the adam." This is a significant interpretive difference: the LXX produces a retributive reading without the specification that human agency (human courts, human execution) is the instrument of justice. The chiastic literary structure is flattened in the LXX rendering.

---

### On 9:7 — The Re-Issued Commission: Framing the Covenant

**9:7** repeats the fruitful-and-multiply mandate of 9:1, creating a bracket around vv. 2-6. The material in vv. 2-6 (the grant of food, the blood prohibition, the blood-for-blood principle) is thus framed between two re-issuances of the creation mandate. The structure is:

- v. 1: Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth [re-creation mandate]
- vv. 2-6: The new post-flood regulations (fear/dread, meat-eating, blood prohibition, capital principle)
- v. 7: Be fruitful and multiply / swarm on the earth and multiply on it [re-creation mandate, amplified]

The addition of שִׁרְצוּ בָאָרֶץ ("swarm upon the earth") in v. 7 adds the creation language of Genesis 1:20-21 (the swarming of creatures). The humans are now commissioned to "swarm" like the creatures — filling, proliferating, covering the earth. The word שָׁרַץ (sharats) is otherwise applied to animals; applying it to humans here is emphatic and perhaps startling: the post-flood population is to spread with creaturely abundance.

---

### On 9:8-17 — The Berit with Noah: The Rainbow Covenant

**בְּרִית (berit, "covenant")** appears seven times in vv. 8-17 (vv. 9, 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 17) — the first time it appears in the biblical narrative with an explicit sign (אוֹת, 'ot). The covenant structure is:

| Element | Verse |
|---|---|
| Parties: God + Noah + sons + all living creatures | 9:9-10 |
| Content: no more flood | 9:11 |
| Sign: the bow (rainbow) | 9:12-13 |
| Mechanism: God will see the bow and remember | 9:14-16 |
| Restatement and seal | 9:17 |

**"Establishing my covenant" (מֵקִים אֶת-בְּרִיתִי, v. 9):** The verb קוּם (qum, "to rise, stand, establish") with בְּרִית means to ratify or confirm a covenant. The Hiphil form הֵקִים means "to cause to stand, to establish." This is distinguished from כָּרַת בְּרִית ("to cut a covenant"), which is the more common covenant-making formula involving the cutting of animals (Gen. 15). Here there is no cutting; the covenant is established by divine declaration and sealed with a natural sign.

**The scope of this covenant:** This is the widest covenant in the Bible. The parties are: Noah, his sons, their descendants, and "every living being of all flesh" — every creature on earth (v. 10). It is a covenant with all creation, not merely with Israel or even with humanity. The rainbow is the sign of God's commitment to the whole created order. This universal scope is theologically unique in the biblical covenant tradition.

---

### On 9:13 — אֶת-קַשְׁתִּי נָתַתִּי בֶּעָנָן: "My Bow I Have Set in the Cloud"

The word קֶשֶׁת (qeshet) is the ordinary Hebrew word for a warrior's bow — a weapon. The use of the same word for the rainbow is not a coincidence of translation; it is the same word. The "rainbow" as a distinct object has no separate vocabulary in Hebrew; it is simply "the bow" (הַקֶּשֶׁת). This linguistic fusion is theologically charged: many interpreters have read the bow set in the cloud as the warrior's bow hung up, pointed skyward — the weapon of divine judgment laid aside, its string released, pointing not at the earth but away from it. If the flood was God's act of judgment (his bow drawn against the earth), then the bow "set in the cloud" after the flood is the weapon hung on the wall — the sign that the battle is over, the judgment has been executed and will not be repeated.

**The bow in Ancient Near Eastern iconography:** Warrior-deities in Mesopotamian iconography were commonly depicted with the bow as their primary weapon of cosmic power. The god Marduk, for example, received a special bow as his weapon in the Enuma Elish. Setting the bow in the heavens is a divine gesture with clear warrior-deity resonance. The Hebrew uses this culturally familiar imagery to make a theological reversal: the bow in the heavens is not the weapon drawn for battle but the weapon set aside as a pledge of peace.

**"I have set" (נָתַתִּי, perfect tense):** The verb is a perfect — "I have set / I set." This may indicate that the bow (rainbow) already existed as a natural phenomenon and is now being conscripted as a covenant sign. Or it may be the prophetic/divine perfect: "I hereby set." Either way, the bow did not originate here; what is new is its investiture as covenant sign.

---

### On 9:15-16 — "And I Will Remember": Divine Memory and the Rainbow

**וְזָכַרְתִּי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי (v. 15) — "And I will remember my covenant"**

As in 8:1 (God remembered Noah), the verb זָכַר (zakar) is not the recall of forgotten information but the activation of covenant loyalty. The rainbow does not remind God of something he might forget; it is the appointed moment of divine covenant-engagement: when the cloud comes over the earth and the bow appears, YHWH will "remember" — will turn his covenant-attention toward the earth, will re-engage his commitment not to destroy.

**"And I will look upon it" (וּרְאִיתִיהָ, v. 16):** The bow is for God to look at — not only for humans to see. This is remarkable: the covenant sign is described primarily as something God himself will look at in order to remember. The rainbow is not merely humanity's assurance; it is God's own mnemonic instrument. The Creator of the cosmos has bound himself to a natural phenomenon as the trigger of his own covenant-memory.

**v. 16 — "The everlasting covenant" (בְּרִית עוֹלָם):** This is the first use of the phrase בְּרִית עוֹלָם ("everlasting/eternal covenant") in the Bible. It will recur at key covenantal moments: the covenant with Abraham (Gen. 17:7, 13, 19), the Sabbath (Ex. 31:16), the covenant with Phinehas (Num. 25:13), and ultimately in the prophetic vision of the new covenant (Isa. 55:3; Jer. 32:40; Ezek. 16:60). The Noahic covenant is the archetype: unconditional, universal, eternal, signed with a natural sign that God himself contemplates.

---

### On 9:18-19 — Ham the Father of Canaan: Proleptic Introduction

**"And Ham — he was the father of Canaan" (וְחָם הוּא אֲבִי כְנָעַן):** This parenthetical identification is inserted before the vineyard narrative precisely to orient the reader before the event unfolds. The reader knows in advance that Ham's significance in what follows is as the progenitor of Canaan. The curse that falls on Canaan (vv. 25-27) is thus telegraphed structurally: Ham is identified as Canaan's father before he acts, so that when the curse on Canaan is pronounced, the reader understands the line of descent.

**"The whole earth was scattered" (נָפְצָה כָל-הָאָרֶץ, v. 19):** The verb נָפַץ (nafats, "to scatter, disperse, shatter") — this will become a key verb in the Tower of Babel narrative (11:4, 8, 9), where the scattering is associated with divine judgment. Here (9:19) the same scattering from Noah's three sons is a neutral or positive statement — the filling of the earth from the three families. The juxtaposition is deliberate: the same verb used here for the natural spread of Noah's descendants will be used in Genesis 11 for the forced scattering of the Babel-builders. The two scatterings stand in contrast.

---

### On 9:20 — וַיָּחֶל נֹחַ אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה: "And Noah Began — a Man of the Ground"

וַיָּחֶל נֹחַ אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה וַיִּטַּע כָּרֶם — "And Noah began — a man of the ground — and he planted a vineyard."

**The syntax of this verse is unusual and debated:**

The phrase אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה ("a man of the ground/earth") appears to be appositional: "Noah, a man of the ground, began." But what did he begin? The verb וַיָּחֶל (vayachel) from the root חָלַל (chalal, "to begin, to profane/defile") is capable of two readings:

1. **חָלַל I** (to begin): "And Noah began — a man of the ground — and he planted a vineyard." This is the dominant reading, treating וַיָּחֶל as from חָלַל ("to begin, inaugurate"). The clause אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה then identifies Noah's vocation: he is a farmer, a man of the soil, and he began his agricultural work by planting a vineyard.

2. **חָלַל II** (to profane/defile): "And Noah, a man of the ground, defiled himself and planted a vineyard." This reading (adopted by some rabbinic interpreters) sees the opening verb as indicating the beginning of Noah's moral declension — his "profaning" of himself through wine-cultivation. This reading would make the planting of the vineyard itself the first act of his moral failure.

The EMET translation follows reading 1 ("began") as the more grammatically natural and contextually appropriate rendering, but notes the ambiguity. The Masoretic vocalization (וַיָּחֶל, from חָלַל I, "to begin") supports reading 1.

**"A man of the ground" (אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה):** This echoes the earlier designation of Cain as an אִישׁ עֹבֵד אֲדָמָה ("a man working the ground," Gen. 4:2). Noah and Cain share the occupation of ground-worker. The association may be deliberate: the ground-worker who brings a produce offering to God (Cain's grain; Noah's vineyard via wine) finds himself in a narratively charged relationship with the earth and its fruits. Noah's vineyard is an agricultural success — the earth is producing again after the flood — but it leads directly to the episode of degradation in vv. 21-27.

**LXX 9:20:** καὶ ἤρξατο Νωε ἄνθρωπος γεωργὸς γῆς — "And Noah began, a man farmer of the earth." The LXX adds γεωργός ("farmer, cultivator") to ἄνθρωπος ("man") — rendering the phrase as "a man who is a farmer of the earth." This preserves the occupational identity. The LXX uses ἤρξατο (from ἄρχω, "to begin") — firmly supporting reading 1 of וַיָּחֶל.

---

### On 9:21-23 — The Vineyard Incident: What Did Ham Do?

**9:21 — וַיִּתְגַּל בְּתוֹךְ אָהֳלֹה: "And He Uncovered Himself within His Tent"**

The verb וַיִּתְגַּל (vayitgal) is the Hithpael of גָּלָה (galah, "to uncover, reveal, go into exile"). Hithpael with a reflexive force: "he uncovered himself," meaning he lay naked. The nakedness (עֶרְוָה, ervah) within the tent is the state that Ham encounters.

**9:22 — The nature of Ham's offense:**

וַיַּרְא חָם אֵת עֶרְוַת אָבִיו — "And Ham saw the nakedness of his father."

The text says only that Ham "saw" (וַיַּרְא) his father's nakedness and then went and told his brothers outside. This has generated enormous interpretive debate. The major proposals:

1. **Simple voyeurism:** Ham saw his father naked (accidental or deliberate) and instead of covering him (the action Shem and Japheth take), he went and told his brothers — mocking, broadcasting the shame. The offense is the combination of seeing and telling rather than covering.

2. **Sexual violation:** Some interpreters (ancient and modern) read "seeing the nakedness" as a euphemism for sexual violation — the same phrase עֶרְוַת אָבִיו in Leviticus 18:8 refers to sexual relations with the father's wife. On this reading, Ham committed a sexual act against his unconscious father. The severity of the curse would support a severe offense.

3. **Castration:** A rabbinic tradition (b. Sanhedrin 70a) reads Ham's action as castrating Noah — explaining why the curse falls on Canaan (Ham's fourth son; Ham had already had three sons and is punished in the fourth, preventing a fourth for Noah).

4. **Incest with Noah's wife:** Another reading connects "uncovering the father's nakedness" (Lev. 18:8: "the nakedness of your father's wife is your father's nakedness") to Ham having intercourse with his mother. Canaan would then be the offspring of this union, explaining why the curse falls on Canaan specifically.

**The EMET translation is deliberately literal:** "And Ham saw — the father of Canaan — the nakedness of his father, and he told to his two brothers outside." The parenthetical "the father of Canaan" is repeated here (as in v. 18) at the moment of the act — the narrator inserts the genealogical consequence into the narrative of the cause. The offense is rendered with maximum fidelity to the Hebrew surface.

**9:23 — Shem and Japheth's Response:**

וַיֵּלְכוּ אֲחֹרַנִּית וַיְכַסּוּ אֵת עֶרְוַת אָבִיהֶם וּפְנֵיהֶם אֲחֹרַנִּית — "And they walked backward and they covered the nakedness of their father; and their faces were backward."

The twice-stated "backward" (אֲחֹרַנִּית) is emphatic. The narrative goes out of its way to specify not merely that they covered Noah but that they did so without looking — walking in backward, with faces averted. The contrast with Ham is complete: Ham looked and told; Shem and Japheth averted their eyes and covered. The honor/shame dynamic is precisely delineated.

---

### On 9:24-27 — The Curse of Canaan and the Blessing of Shem

**9:24 — "His youngest son" (בְּנוֹ הַקָּטָן):**

Noah awakes and "knows" what "his youngest son" (בְּנוֹ הַקָּטָן) did to him. In the standard ordering of Noah's sons — Shem, Ham, Japheth — Ham is the middle son, not the youngest. Japheth is listed last in several passages (Gen. 5:32; 6:10; 7:13; 9:18) though 10:21 calls Shem "the elder brother of Japheth," implying Japheth is younger. The identification of בְּנוֹ הַקָּטָן is therefore ambiguous:
- If Ham is the youngest, the standard birth-order is being re-arranged.
- If Japheth is the youngest and the text means that Ham's offense was reported to Noah via the response of his youngest son (Japheth helping Shem), this is a strained reading.
- Most plausibly, "youngest" here refers to Ham in the context of this specific family unit — Ham being younger than Shem.

**9:25 — "Cursed be Canaan" — not Ham:**

The curse falls on Canaan, not on Ham. This has puzzled interpreters for millennia. The most common explanations:
1. **Principle of talion by proxy:** Ham sinned against his father; Ham's son is cursed — the punishment is generational, falling on the next generation, mirroring the structure of sins of fathers affecting children (Ex. 20:5).
2. **Ham as already blessed:** God blessed Noah's sons in 9:1; a divine blessing cannot be reversed, so Ham cannot be directly cursed; his seed (Canaan) is cursed instead.
3. **Narrative purpose:** The curse on Canaan serves the Israel-Canaan polemic. The Canaanites (Israel's great enemies and the inhabitants of the Promised Land) are here given an origin curse, providing theological justification for Israelite displacement of Canaanites. This is a later ideological layer visible in the text.
4. **Historical memory:** Some scholars read Canaan as originally the name of the offending son in an earlier version of the story, with Ham being a later substitution to systematize the Table of Nations structure.

**"A slave of slaves" (עֶבֶד עֲבָדִים):** This is a Hebrew superlative construction — the repetition of a noun in genitive for intensification: the most enslaved of slaves, the absolute slave. Compare "song of songs" (שִׁיר הַשִּׁירִים, the greatest song), "holy of holies" (קֹדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים, the most holy place), "vanity of vanities" (הֲבֵל הֲבָלִים).

**9:26 — "Blessed be YHWH, the God of Shem":**

The blessing of Shem is remarkable in its indirect form. Noah does not say "Blessed be Shem" (as one might expect, parallel to "Cursed be Canaan") but "Blessed be YHWH, the God of Shem." Shem is blessed through his God: the blessing accrues to Shem because YHWH is his God, not because of anything inherent in Shem himself. The divine name YHWH appears here for the first time since 8:21 — and it is used precisely to identify YHWH as the particular God of Shem's lineage. This is the first intimation that YHWH's covenant relationship will run through the Shem-line — the line from which Eber (the eponymous ancestor of the Hebrews) and eventually Abram/Abraham will descend (Gen. 11:10-26). The blessing of Shem is in reality the first anticipation of the election of Israel.

**9:27 — יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים לְיֶפֶת: The Japheth Wordplay**

יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים לְיֶפֶת — "God shall enlarge Japheth [יֶפֶת]."

The verb יַפְתְּ is from פָּתָה / פָּתַח (to open, expand, enlarge) — forming a clear wordplay on the name יֶפֶת (Japheth). As Noah (נֹחַ) and the verb נוּחַ ("rest") are linked throughout the flood narrative, so Japheth (יֶפֶת) and the verb יַפְתְּ ("shall enlarge") are etymologically linked. The blessing is performative and onomastic: Japheth will be what his name implies — expansive, wide-spreading. The Table of Nations in Genesis 10 reflects this: Japheth's descendants occupy the widest geographic spread (the coastlands, the Greeks, the Anatolians, the Medes, etc.).

**"He shall dwell in the tents of Shem" (וְיִשְׁכֹּן בְּאָהֳלֵי-שֵׁם):** The subject of וְיִשְׁכֹּן ("he shall dwell") is ambiguous — it could be Japheth or God. Two readings:
1. **Japheth shall dwell in the tents of Shem** — Japheth's expansion will bring him into the territory of Shem; the peoples of Japheth will live among the peoples of Shem.
2. **God shall dwell in the tents of Shem** — continuing the subject "God" from the previous clause (יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים). God will dwell in Shem's line — an anticipation of the divine presence dwelling with Israel (Shem's descendants).

The ambiguity is probably intentional. The EMET translation follows the grammatical flow: the last explicit subject is "God" (אֱלֹהִים), so "he shall dwell" most naturally refers to God. However, the majority of traditional translations follow reading 1 (Japheth as subject). EMET notes this without resolving it.

**LXX 9:27:** πλατύναι ὁ θεὸς τῷ Ιαφεθ — "May God enlarge for Japheth." The LXX uses the optative form (may he enlarge) rather than the future indicative, giving the blessing a wish-character. The LXX then: καὶ κατοικησάτω ἐν τοῖς οἴκοις τοῦ Σημ — "and let him dwell in the houses of Shem" — using the 3rd person singular optative κατοικησάτω. The LXX subject here is ambiguous in the same way as the MT.

---

### On 9:28-29 — Noah's Death Notice: The Generations Formula

וַיְחִי-נֹחַ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַחֲמִשִּׁים שָׁנָה — "And Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years."

Noah's post-flood life (350 years) added to his pre-flood life (600 years at the time of the flood, Gen. 7:6) equals his total of 950 years. The death notice uses the standard Priestly genealogical formula that was employed throughout Genesis 5 for the antediluvian patriarchs: "all the days of [Name] were X years and he died" (וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל-יְמֵי X שָׁנָה וַיָּמֹת). Noah's death notice closes the account that began in Genesis 5:32 (the first mention of Noah as a son of Lamech). He is the last of the antediluvian patriarchs to die; with his death, the primordial world of Genesis 1-9 fully closes and the Table of Nations (Genesis 10) and the Babel episode (Genesis 11) belong to a new era.

**Noah's total lifespan of 950 years** is the second-longest in the antediluvian/transitional lists, exceeded only by Methuselah's 969 years (Gen. 5:27). He outlives the flood by 350 years — suggesting that in the narrative's logic, he witnesses the full spread of his sons' families across the earth, the planting of the new civilization, before he dies.

**The absence of a burial notice:** Unlike Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and others in Genesis, Noah receives no burial notice. The text simply closes: "and he died." The stark finality is consistent with the primordial character of the narrative — the highly formal, formulaic close of the antediluvian era with no lingering geographical or genealogical detail about burial.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 9

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9:1 | פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ וּמִלְאוּ אֶת-הָאָרֶץ | αὐξάνεσθε καὶ πληθύνεσθε καὶ πληρώσατε τὴν γῆν | Faithful repetition of the Gen 1:28 commission; LXX uses same verbs as its 1:28 rendering |
| 9:2 | מוֹרַאֲכֶם וְחִתְּכֶם ("your fear and your dread") | ὁ τρόμος ὑμῶν καὶ ὁ φόβος ("your trembling and your fear") | LXX reverses the pairing; φόβος (fear) and τρόμος (trembling/dread); both preserve the hendiadys |
| 9:4 | בְּנַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ ("in its life — its blood") | ἐν αἵματι ψυχῆς ("in the blood of life") | LXX syntactically reverses the apposition: "blood of life" rather than "life — blood"; loses the blood-nephesh identification |
| 9:6 | בָּאָדָם דָּמוֹ יִשָּׁפֵךְ ("by the adam his blood shall be shed") | ἀντὶ τοῦ αἵματος αὐτοῦ ἐκχυθήσεται ("in return for his blood it shall be poured") | LXX reads בָּ as retributive ("in return for") rather than agentive ("by means of"); loses the chiastic structure |
| 9:13 | אֶת-קַשְׁתִּי נָתַתִּי בֶּעָנָן ("my bow I have set in the cloud") | τὸ τόξον μου τίθημι ἐν τῇ νεφέλῃ ("my bow I set in the cloud") | LXX uses the present tense τίθημι ("I set/place") rather than the perfect — making the act present/ongoing rather than a completed setting |
| 9:20 | וַיָּחֶל נֹחַ ("and Noah began") | καὶ ἤρξατο Νωε ("and Noah began") | LXX confirms reading of וַיָּחֶל as "began" (ἄρχω), not "defiled" |
| 9:20 | אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה ("a man of the ground") | ἄνθρωπος γεωργὸς γῆς ("a man, farmer of the earth") | LXX adds γεωργός (farmer/agriculturalist), making the occupation explicit |
| 9:25 | עֶבֶד עֲבָדִים יִהְיֶה לְאֶחָיו ("a slave of slaves shall he be to his brothers") | παῖς οἰκέτης ἔσται τοῖς ἀδελφοῖς αὐτοῦ ("a servant-slave shall he be to his brothers") | LXX does not use the Hebrew superlative doublet; renders as παῖς οἰκέτης (a household servant/slave) |
| 9:27 | יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים לְיֶפֶת ("God shall enlarge Japheth") | πλατύναι ὁ θεὸς τῷ Ιαφεθ ("may God enlarge for Japheth") | LXX uses optative (wish/prayer) rather than declarative future; the blessing becomes a wish rather than a pronouncement |

---

### Textual Variants and DSS

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** No significant Genesis 9 fragments are extant from Qumran. The Damascus Document (CD) and other Qumran texts cite the Noahic blood prohibition in ethical contexts, but do not provide a variant text of Genesis 9 itself.

**Samaritan Pentateuch:** The Samaritan Pentateuch (SP) is largely in agreement with the MT for Genesis 9. Minor orthographic differences only; no significant variant readings that affect translation.

**9:20 — Peshitta (Syriac):** The Syriac Peshitta renders וַיָּחֶל נֹחַ as "and Noah was humbled / debased" — following the reading of חָלַל II ("to profane/defile"), treating the planting of the vineyard as the beginning of Noah's self-abasement. This is a minority interpretive tradition, and the LXX's ἤρξατο ("began") and the Targum's rendering support reading I (began).

**9:27 — Subject of "he shall dwell":** The ambiguity of the subject is preserved in all ancient versions (LXX, Vulgate, Peshitta, Targum Onkelos). This is a genuine crux of the text.
